speaker: interviewer
text: Coleman. I'd like to go back a little to Auschwitz, when you were traveling between the camps. You had mentioned that you were in several of the different camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Auschwitz, I didn't travel. But once I was moved to Birkenau--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --then we traveled between camp. But I want to mention, being in Auschwitz was a month, was in 1943, February. The, the head of the block, which is the German who was in the head of the block, Olshevsky, told us to undress completely, and made us run to the showers, which is about a city block. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We, we came into the shower and there was cold water. And they let us stay there for about five or six minutes, and then they chased us back out. There was-- must have been outside, freezing, because when I came back to the block, I had chunks of ice on my body. And in fact, one of the, one of them young men fell right dead there, on the run. Then, when we came back to the block, they made us-- he made us sit on the beds, naked, with the windows open on all sides for about two hours. Then they gave us the clothes. And things like this happened on and off in Auschwitz, which I don't recall any of-- every little incident, but there were many incidents of beating, and for little-- any little thing. One day, I remember, while I was working still in Auschwitz, we were putting up on the remaining one-story building, we were putting up a second floor. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Polacks sent me to the kitchen, to the camp kitchen. And they told me-- they gave me a pail, and clay, and tools, and a rag. And I should go into the camp kitchen, in one of the rooms where the-- they had basins with peeled potatoes for the SS. And there's an oven there, and I should-- there's like, gratings. I should break the gratings with a hammer. And they gave me new gratings, and I should put in new gratings. But while I'm doing with this, I should put in potatoes on the bottom of the pail, and cover it with, with the rag, and the the clay-- whatever's left of the clay on top, and the tools, and walk out. The problem was that the SS in there were walking around everywhere, you know, And in the doorway, as you walked out, the SS men stood also.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I luckily passed him by. You know, he didn't look inside the pail. But they made me do a lot of those things. One time, a truck that brought in the bread to camp had a, a big [? tarpark, ?] you know? Covered with a [? tarpark. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they told me I should jump up on the truck, and stay there. And take a couple loaves of bread, and then jump out and run back to the building where, where I'm working. Or they made me-- they used to do-- they, they used to-- they allowed-- they allowed them to have an oven made out of a-- like a primitive oven, out, out of a barrel. But what they did is, they put some grating inside, and they used to cook inside. So they made me stay-- but they weren't allowed to do it. But they told me to stay outside and watch, like a watchman. See if anyone coming, either SS or anybody from the camp. But if they saw you standing there doing nothing, you were, you were, you were punished too. So they made me do a lot of things, you know, where I jeopardized my life, but naturally, they didn't care about it. And-- We-- there's so many things, you know, that happened that it's impossible to remember. Where you, you get like a mental block at certain times, where you try to remember. And then after a while, when you get together with people who you were there, you, you remember after you talk, and you get reminded of certain incidents. And then you, you bring in, you come in with a lot of things that you remember. But in general, it's very, very hard to remember. When we were moved from Auschwitz to Birkenau, I worked in the gypsy camp many days, on and off. And it was so beautiful there. Even they were incarcerated, but they were with their families, with their children, with their babies. They had babies in there too. And we-- I used to talk to them. And I, I, I enjoyed being there, because, you know, I missed so much of my own family. [CRYING] But after a while, naturally, they were all put into the gas chambers. And it was only a matter of days. Everyone knew there was only a matter of days till our own time will come. Many days you walked out into the camp, and the fences were all electrified. And you saw people hanging on the, on the, on the fence who committed suicide. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Many times, you know, you were thinking of doing the same thing because, you said to yourself, what is the reason to, to suffer? What is the reason to survive? You're not going to survive. The end result is going to be, you're going to go where all the other people go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember cases when I came first to Birkenau, and they had only one crematorium. And what they did in the beginning, is after they gassed the people, they threw them into ditches, and they were burning the people. And some people were alive, you know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I will never forget this.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] I have seen an SS man. I remember his face every time I think about it. He took a little baby by his feet, and he hit it with the head against the tree. And then he threw it into the ditch. And then, naturally, later on-- you know, the smell-- when they had those ditches, the smell was so terrible. You couldn't breathe. There was smoke all over. The camp was like, dark from smoke. And you seen the fire, you know, you also seen the fire from the crematoriums, you know, when they already used the crematoriums. [CRYING] And many times, you know, when I was working there, near the crematoriums, or inside the crematoriums, and I saw the people walking there, with the families, with children, old men, young men. [CRYING] Young women. [SOBS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ready to go and tell them what's expecting, what's-- where they were going. Because they were told, oh, they're going to the showers, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in there, in the gas chambers, many times, because we used to work down there too. We used to do like, cement work. And you know, they really had shower heads there. And people, you know, many times, I figured, when I saw them walking there, and they were waiting for the water to come in. [CRYING] But I, I assume that once they saw that they went in there with the men and women together, with the [INAUDIBLE], they-- I assume people gathered already, what there end is. But you know-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --when you are in a surrounding like this, and you see, every day, what's happening, somehow, you get accustomed. Your heart becomes like a stone. And you live with it. Or when they brought the Czechoslovakian families from Theresienstadt, beautiful people, beautiful kids-- because I used to go in there. I had the chance to go into all these camps. And I knew what the end result is going to be, because I knew they're not going to let them live, especially with families. But I thought maybe they're going to let the young people stay, or the young women, and they are going to put them to work. But it wasn't the case. They eliminated the whole camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- after that, they brought in Hungarian Jews, women, into that camp. And they tortured them so much, you know? They kept them naked, outside the camp there, on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also remember an incident about being in Auschwitz. It was in 1943. They brought in a town's worth of Greek Jews, and they brought in about 20 Greek Jews-- [COUGHS] --to my place, to my commander there. [COUGHS] To my place of work. [COUGHS] And they were beaten and kicked for one reason, they couldn't understand the language. They didn't know how to speak. They don't know how to speak either German, Jewish, or, or, or any language. That's all they knew, is Greek, or, or some of them knew Hebrew. Well, I, like-- [COUGHS] I had a man, which [INAUDIBLE], a Polish man, which was the architect of the, of the construction, with the name of Vladistok Smalsky And he was heading the construction work. And he took a liking to me because of the similar numbers that we had. The first four numbers of his were similar to mine. So I begged him, I said to him, do me a favor. I said, let me take care of these Greek Jews. I said, I know how to converse with them. I speak Hebrew, and I could-- and I could tell them what to do. Just tell me what you want them to do, and I tell them what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And why should they be beaten and kicked, you know? Because they don't understand the language. They don't understand what they were told to do. So he agreed. He said, OK, because he was in charge there too, you know? He had a big say in the place there. And I explained to them in Hebrew, because not all of them spoke Hebrew, but a few of them spoke Hebrew. There were a few young men there and a couple older men also. So they-- after we finished the building-- not completely finished, but after we did most of the cement work-- they put them up to do the windows, to clean the windows. And they did clean the windows. And then they put them in our own-- in, in my block, there on my, on my room there where I was, that big room, you know? And Saturdays-- on Sundays-- we didn't work on Sundays-- on Sundays, they'd come over to me, and they brought me their portions of bread, you know? And I told them not to give it to me. I says, I don't want it. You know, because you need it, because, you know, if you want to survive, you'll need that and much more, you know? But they were so grateful to me, you know? And they were kissing me. But then after a while, you know, we were transferred to Birkenau, and I never heard from them anymore. So I don't know what happened to them, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But being in Birkenau, I've seen so many atrocities. The women, you know, young women, walking around, half dead. Some of them were like half dead. How they could walk anymore. And once the took him on the [? revere ?], which is called-- the hospital calls [? revere ?] in German. They were put to death by injection. Or, if there was enough, they took them to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, my brother, my oldest brother-- which had an accident on his-- on his place of work, where he worked-- I was told it by somebody, you know? He went to the hospital, and they injected him with a deadly injection. And how he was-- that's how he died in camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had more opportunities to see more than any other-- well, there were some, you know, which had similar work of mine, but very few. But, like I said, I walked every camp, sometimes three or four times, to different camps. And I've seen so many terrible things. And I looked at the women, you know, they were like skeletons, you know, walking around. Live skeletons. [CRYING] And then in that gypsy camp, you know, we already heard, you know, that they going to be destroyed. But I didn't have the heart to tell them this.

speaker: interviewee
text: They thought that they were going to live here until the war ends, you know? And then the Germans were going to-- that's what they were told. They're going to set them up in certain areas, you know? They're going to put them to work. But they didn't do any work at all. And you know, I've seen so many beautiful faces. Young girls, so beautiful. They were always saying gypsies are beautiful, but they were really so beautiful, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we got up-- when we-- that night, when we heard where-- the way they [? emptying ?] up because, you know, our barracks-- you know, between-- the distance between their camp and our camp, which we were in, was like from this wall to this wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we could hear everything. And you know, talking to ourselves, you know, at night, we just said, you know, that today them. Maybe next we will-- next is our turn. And that's how we were thinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting many times-- whenever they'd let us sit, you know, for lunch time, to have that cup of soup, that bowl of soup-- we were reminiscing about home, about families. One was richer, one was poorer, but we all had brothers. We all had sisters. We had uncles. We had aunts. We had so many big families.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a happy life. And here we are sitting and waiting to die. Why? What for? Many times, you know, we were debating. Why? Why not go and hold the electric wire and finish with it? That's all, because we're going to be finished anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, look, I, I found out that the drag of life is so strong. The will of life is so strong, that you're ready to overcome anything, the worst, to live, to survive. And we did that. And thanks to God that I survived, and I found my wife after the war, when she was engaged to my older brother. She was in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came home after liberation. I came home, and I, I knew that my family is all dead. But still, you figure, maybe. I know my sisters were gassed. My brother was killed. My two brothers were shot. My father was shot. My mother was shot. But still, I believed. And I went home to my hometown, and I had two girls with me, which were in the hospital with me. One-- [CLEARS THROAT] One was from Vilna and one was from Romania. Cluj Klausenburg. Her name was Terry Rosenberg. And the other girl's name-- I don't know. Her last name, I don't know. But her first name was Nellie. She was from Vilna. She's still alive. I see her in Israel a few times. Her husband passed away also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came home to a not very good surrounding. And I met-- so I came straight to my house, to my parents' house. There was a Polish man living inside from a village somewhere, from a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in. I told him, I says, this is my parents' house. We lived here. I was born here. My-- so he's laughing at me. He says the government gave him the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, but you go. There's a big building not too far from here. There's the headquarters of the Communist party, and the guy by the name Mehler. And he will give you quarters. I knew I heard the name Mehler because my father's brother-- not the youngest, but next to him-- was a lawyer. But his license was taken away from him because he was a little leftist, you know? He belonged to the [? poaletzion ?]-- but not the right, but the left. And we had a-- like a, not a burgermeister, but higher than that, you know? And he was-- I don't know whether you heard. There was a pogrom in Poland before the war in Przytyk. There was a pogrom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yeah, there was a big pogrom at Przytyk, and he was the head of that city. And then he was transferred to our city. So this burgermeister-- I'll call him burgermeister-- took the license away from my uncle because May 1st, they hung out a red flag. So he was not allowed to practice law.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 1st of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 1st is the labor, labor holiday they celebrate in Russia. They also celebrate all over Europe and all socialist countries. So May 1st they used to hang up from their place where they had their, their rooms. They hung up a red flag. And actually, with the Star of David, but it was a red flag. And so he was stopped from practicing the law. So my sister used to help him. She used to-- whenever these-- farmers, mostly, used to come to him. So they told him their, their [INAUDIBLE], I mean their, their story, and their case. And my sister used to type it on a typewriter-- my oldest sister. And then they went with that court.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went down to that building to see that man, Mehler And he knew my uncle very well because he himself was a communist. He was already sitting in Poland before the war and, and imprisoned for being a communist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in there and I told him who-- because I was a kid, so he didn't know me-- but I told him my name. And he says, oh, yeah. I knew your uncle. I knew your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- he's said to him-- he said to me that he can put me up here in the building. But I said, I have two girls with me also. He says, well, he's going to put them up, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after I rest up for two weeks, he's going to send me to an officer school, and he's going to make me a, a lieutenant of a section of the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he mentioned to me, UB. And I said, I don't know what UB means. UB. U-B. In Polish it's UB. And I said, what is UB? And he said UB is like-- he asked me if I know what NKVD is. And I says, NKVD I heard. That's the secret police in Russia. So I said, well, I said-- after I heard that, I said-- tell me something. Are there any other Jews in town? He says, yeah, there are two more Jews in town. He said there were two Jews that were in hiding with the girl. She's a [INAUDIBLE] Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went up there with the girls, but they didn't have any room for the two girls. So I went to a woman who used to clean in our house. And I promised her as soon as I get some money together, I'll pay you for it, so they can sleep in her apartment. She had an apartment. And I stayed with them, and I provided them. And now, they didn't have any food, so I provided them with food, because I used to go out in the villages and organize food. But I couldn't stay there too long because they-- every time I used to walk into a place, they were talking that I-- I'm in secret police. And I-- in the interim I found out that my my, brother's wife-- or, no. Her-- his, his-- he wasn't married yet-- his girlfriend is in Lodz. And I went and I got her there, And I brought her back home with me. And we got married, but we had to leave because I was afraid of what the talk is going on in town. And then we heard there's a pogrom being ready. It was Slavic. They, they-- there was a, a, a gossip going around. That was a bit before Passover in 1945. There were gossip going around that a, a, a Christian child disappeared, and already the Jews are going to use the blood for matzos. And we already heard the, the, the news.

speaker: interviewee
text: But eventually they found-- there was a Jewish head of the police there, you know? They found the kid. They were hiding him in a basement. And they wanted to take me to the Polish army, and I decided I'm not going to stay in the Polish army four more years. And we left by train to [? Doushnikis drouie ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's two aunts, which also survived Birkenau, one of them passed away. The other one is still in Australia. In, in-- not Sydney, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Canberra?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Melbourne. In Melbourne, right. And we stayed there, staying by the Czechoslovakian border. They were living there, the two sisters. They both married. And we stayed there for a couple of weeks. And then we decided to go to a travel the border to Czechoslovakia. We crossed a border to Czechoslovakia. They, they were checking us, if we carry any contraband, or gold, or diamonds. And we came in to Austria, to Vienna. From there on the Jewish agency, or the Bricha-- were put in a, a kaserne, which is an army-- like an army building. There were about, maybe, 2,000 people. And we stayed there. And we-- they fed us. There was a public kitchen. And we stayed for 10 weeks, but my wife got sick right after the war. She was, in fact, in my home town, she was in the hospital. And she got sick there, too. She was in the Rothschild Hospital in Vienna for two or three weeks, and then we decided to go to Germany.
